subject: Physics
solution:
The formula to calculate the Young's modulus of the material of the wire, in the first case, can be written as

Y=Fπr2lL   ...1

From equation (1), it follows that

F=Yπr2×lL   ...2

For the second case, the Young's modulus is given by

Y=F/2πr2/4l'L   ...3

From equation (3), it follows that

F=Yl'L×2×πr24   ...4

From equations (2) and (4), it implies that

Yπr2×lL=Yl'L×2×πr24⇒l'=2l

subject: Physics
solution:
Given, $y=2 \times 10^{11} \mathrm{Nm}^{-2}$
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \text { Stress }\left(\frac{\mathrm{F}}{\mathrm{A}}\right)=5 \times 10^7 \mathrm{Nm}^{-2} \\
& \Delta \mathrm{V}=0.02 \%=2 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~m}^3 \\
& \frac{\Delta \mathrm{r}}{\mathrm{r}}=?
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \gamma=\frac{\text { stress }}{\text { strain }} \Rightarrow \operatorname{strain}\left(\frac{\Delta \ell}{\ell_0}\right)=\frac{\gamma}{\text { stress }} \\
& \Delta \mathrm{V}=2 \pi \mathrm{r} \ell_0 \Delta \mathrm{r}-\pi \mathrm{r}^2 \Delta \ell
\end{aligned}
$$
From eqns (i) and (ii) putting the value of $\Delta \ell, \ell_0$ and $\Delta \mathrm{V}$ and solving we get
$$
\frac{\Delta \mathrm{r}}{\mathrm{r}}=0.25 \times 10^{-4}
$$

solution: $\begin{aligned} & 3=\mathrm{K}(\mathrm{a}-\ell) \\ & 2=\mathrm{K}(\mathrm{b}-\ell) \\ & \mathrm{T}=\mathrm{K}(3 \mathrm{a}-2 \mathrm{~b}-\ell) \\ & \mathrm{T}=\mathrm{K}(3(\mathrm{a}-\ell)-2(\mathrm{~b}-\ell) \\ & =\mathrm{K}\left[3\left(\frac{3}{\mathrm{~K}}\right)-2\left(\frac{2}{\mathrm{~K}}\right)\right] \\ & =9-4 \\ & =5 \mathrm{~N}\end{aligned}$

$\begin{aligned} & \ell=2 \mathrm{~m} ; \mathrm{Y}=2 \times 10^{11} \frac{\mathrm{~N}}{\mathrm{~m}^2} \\ & \begin{aligned} \mu=-\frac{\left(\frac{\Delta \mathrm{r}}{\mathrm{r}}\right)}{\left(\frac{\Delta \ell}{\ell}\right)} \Rightarrow \frac{\Delta \ell}{\ell} & =\frac{1}{\mu} \times\left(\frac{\Delta \mathrm{r}}{\mathrm{r}}\right) \\ & =\frac{1}{0.2} \times\left(10^{-3}\right)\end{aligned}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
solution:
Let $T$ be the tension in the wire at a distance $\dfrac{l}{3}$ from the top.

The length of the wire below this point is $l - \dfrac{l}{3} = \dfrac{2l}{3}$.

The weight of this lower part of the wire is $\dfrac{w}{l} \times \dfrac{2l}{3} = \dfrac{2w}{3}$.

The total downward force (tension $T$) at this point is the sum of the weight $W$ attached at the bottom and the weight of the lower part of the wire:

$T = W + \dfrac{2w}{3}$

The stress at this point is given by the tension divided by the cross-sectional area $A$:

$\text{Stress} = \dfrac{T}{A} = \dfrac{W + \dfrac{2w}{3}}{A} = \dfrac{W}{A} + \dfrac{2}{3}\dfrac{w}{A}$

Comparing this with the given expression for stress, $\dfrac{W}{A} + \dfrac{2}{\gamma}\dfrac{w}{A}$, we get:

$\gamma = 3$

Answer: $3$

subject: Physics
solution:
According to questions,
$\begin{aligned}
& \frac{\ell_{\mathrm{s}}}{\ell_{\mathrm{b}}}=\mathrm{a}, \frac{\mathrm{r}_{\mathrm{s}}}{\mathrm{r}_{\mathrm{b}}}=\mathrm{b}, \frac{\mathrm{y}_{\mathrm{s}}}{\mathrm{y}_{\mathrm{b}}}=\mathrm{c}, \frac{\Delta \ell \mathrm{s}}{\Delta \ell_{\mathrm{b}}}=? \\
& \text { As, } \mathrm{y}=\frac{\mathrm{F} \ell}{\mathrm{A} \Delta \ell} \Rightarrow \Delta \ell=\frac{\mathrm{F} \ell}{\mathrm{Ay}} \\
& \Delta \ell_{\mathrm{s}}=\frac{3 \mathrm{mg} \ell_{\mathrm{s}}}{\pi \mathrm{r}_{\mathrm{s}}^2 \cdot \mathrm{y}_{\mathrm{s}}}\left[\because \mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{s}}=(\mathrm{M}+2 \mathrm{M}) \mathrm{g}\right] \\
& \Delta \ell_{\mathrm{b}}=\frac{2 \mathrm{Mg}\ell_{\mathrm{b}}}{\pi \mathrm{r}_{\mathrm{b}}^2 \cdot \mathrm{y}_{\mathrm{b}}}\left[\because \mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{b}}=2 \mathrm{Mg}\right]
\end{aligned}$
$\therefore \frac{\Delta \ell_{\mathrm{s}}}{\Delta \ell_{\mathrm{b}}}=\frac{\frac{3 \mathrm{Mg} \ell_{\mathrm{s}}}{\pi \mathrm{r}_{\mathrm{s}}^2 \cdot \mathrm{y}_{\mathrm{s}}}}{\frac{2 \mathrm{Mg} \cdot \ell_{\mathrm{b}}}{\pi \mathrm{r}_{\mathrm{b}}^2 \cdot \mathrm{y}_{\mathrm{b}}}}=\frac{3 \mathrm{a}}{2 \mathrm{b}^2 \mathrm{C}}$

subject: Physics
solution:
Young's modulus ($Y$) is defined as the ratio of stress to strain: $Y = \frac{\text{stress}}{\text{strain}}$.
In the given graph, the y-axis represents strain and the x-axis represents stress.
The slope of the line in a strain-stress graph is given by $m = \frac{\text{strain}}{\text{stress}}$.
Therefore, $Y = \frac{1}{\text{slope}}$.
To have the largest Young's modulus, the material must have the smallest slope in the strain-stress plot.
From the figure, line $C$ has the smallest angle with the stress axis (x-axis), meaning it has the minimum slope.
Since $Y \propto \frac{1}{\text{slope}}$, material $C$ has the largest Young's modulus.

subject: Physics
solution:
Let the maximum mass that can be added to the pan be $m$.

The breaking stress for both wires is $\sigma_{max} = 12 \times 10^8 \text{ N/m}^2$.

For the upper wire:
Area of cross-section, $A_u = 0.008 \text{ cm}^2 = 8 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^2$
Maximum tension the upper wire can withstand is:
$T_{u,max} = \sigma_{max} \times A_u = (12 \times 10^8) \times (8 \times 10^{-7}) = 960 \text{ N}$
The total mass supported by the upper wire is $(30 + 10 + m) = (40 + m) \text{ kg}$.
Tension in the upper wire, $T_u = (40 + m)g = (40 + m) \times 10$
To avoid breaking the upper wire:
$(40 + m) \times 10 \le 960$
$40 + m \le 96 \implies m \le 56 \text{ kg}$

For the lower wire:
Area of cross-section, $A_l = 0.004 \text{ cm}^2 = 4 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^2$
Maximum tension the lower wire can withstand is:
$T_{l,max} = \sigma_{max} \times A_l = (12 \times 10^8) \times (4 \times 10^{-7}) = 480 \text{ N}$
The total mass supported by the lower wire is $(10 + m) \text{ kg}$.
Tension in the lower wire, $T_l = (10 + m)g = (10 + m) \times 10$
To avoid breaking the lower wire:
$(10 + m) \times 10 \le 480$
$10 + m \le 48 \implies m \le 38 \text{ kg}$

To ensure neither wire breaks, the added mass $m$ must satisfy both conditions. Therefore, the maximum mass that can be added is the smaller of the two values:
$m = 38 \text{ kg}$

Answer: $38$

subject: Physics
solution:
Maximum stress $\sigma_{max} = 4 \times 10^8$ N/m$^2$

Radius of wire $r = 4 \times 10^{-3}$ m

Mass of lift $m = 1600$ kg

Maximum tension the wire can withstand is given by:

$T_{max} = \sigma_{max} \times \pi r^2$

$T_{max} = 4 \times 10^8 \times 3.14 \times (4 \times 10^{-3})^2$

$T_{max} = 4 \times 10^8 \times 3.14 \times 16 \times 10^{-6} = 20096$ N

When the lift accelerates upwards with maximum acceleration $a$, the tension in the wire is:

$T_{max} = m(g + a)$

$20096 = 1600(10 + a)$

$10 + a = \dfrac{20096}{1600} = 12.56$

$a = 12.56 - 10 = 2.56$ m/s$^2$

Answer: $2.56$

subject: Physics
solution:
Given:
Side length of the cube, $L = 5$ cm $= 0.05$ m
Modulus of rigidity, $\eta = 10^5$ N/m$^2$
Force applied on the upper face, $F = 10$ N

Area of the upper face, $A = L^2 = (0.05)^2 = 2.5 \times 10^{-3}$ m$^2$

Shear stress is given by:
$\text{Stress} = \dfrac{F}{A} = \dfrac{10}{2.5 \times 10^{-3}} = 4000$ N/m$^2$

Shear strain is given by:
$\text{Strain} = \dfrac{\Delta x}{L}$

Modulus of rigidity is defined as the ratio of shear stress to shear strain:
$\eta = \dfrac{\text{Stress}}{\text{Strain}} = \dfrac{F/A}{\Delta x/L}$

Rearranging for displacement $\Delta x$:
$\Delta x = \dfrac{F \cdot L}{A \cdot \eta}$

Substituting the values:
$\Delta x = \dfrac{10 \times 0.05}{2.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 10^5}$

$\Delta x = \dfrac{0.5}{250} = 0.002$ m

Converting to millimeters:
$\Delta x = 0.002 \times 1000 = 2$ mm

Answer: $2$

subject: Physics
solution:
Given:
Length of string A, $L_A = 0.314$ m
Young's modulus of string A, $Y_A = 2 \times 10^{10}$ N/m$^2$
Radius of both strings, $r = 0.2$ mm $= 2 \times 10^{-4}$ m
Length of string B, $L_B = 2L_A = 0.628$ m
Young's modulus of string B, $Y_B = 2Y_A = 4 \times 10^{10}$ N/m$^2$
Mass of load, $m = 0.8$ kg
Acceleration due to gravity, $g = 10$ m/s$^2$

Tension in both strings, $T = mg = 0.8 \times 10 = 8$ N

Cross-sectional area of both strings:
$A = \pi r^2 = 3.14 \times (2 \times 10^{-4})^2 = 3.14 \times 4 \times 10^{-8}$ m$^2$

Elongation in string A:
$\Delta L_A = \dfrac{T L_A}{A Y_A}$
$\Delta L_A = \dfrac{8 \times 0.314}{3.14 \times 4 \times 10^{-8} \times 2 \times 10^{10}}$
$\Delta L_A = \dfrac{8 \times 0.314}{8 \times 3.14 \times 10^2} = \dfrac{0.1}{10^2} = 10^{-3}$ m $= 1$ mm

Elongation in string B:
$\Delta L_B = \dfrac{T L_B}{A Y_B}$
$\Delta L_B = \dfrac{8 \times (2 \times 0.314)}{A \times (2 Y_A)} = \dfrac{T L_A}{A Y_A} = 1$ mm

Net change in length of the combination:
$\Delta L = \Delta L_A + \Delta L_B = 1 + 1 = 2$ mm

Answer: $2$

subject: Physics
options: Both (A) and (R) are true and (R) is the correct explanation (A)
Both (A) and (R) are true but (R) is not the correct explanation of (A)
solution:
Elasticity is the ability of a material to deform under stress and then return to its original shape when the stress is removed.

The restoring force in elastic materials is associated with the inter-atomic or inter-molecular forces that act to bring the atoms or molecules back to their original positions when the material is deformed. When an external force is applied to a material, it causes a displacement of atoms or molecules from their equilibrium positions. The restoring force is a result of the potential energy stored in these inter-atomic or inter-molecular bonds, and it works to bring the material back to its original state.

Hence, both (A) and (R) are true and (R) is the correct explanation (A)

subject: Physics
solution:
The formula for Young's modulus is given by:

$Y = \dfrac{F L_0}{A \Delta L} = \dfrac{4 F L_0}{\pi d^2 \Delta L}$

Substituting the given values into the formula:

$Y = \dfrac{4 \times 100 \times 1.5}{\pi \times (0.08 \times 10^{-2})^2 \times (0.5 \times 10^{-2})}$

$Y = \dfrac{600}{\pi \times 64 \times 10^{-8} \times 5 \times 10^{-3}} = \dfrac{15}{8\pi} \times 10^{11} \text{ N/m}^2$

The relative error in Young's modulus is:

$\dfrac{\Delta Y}{Y} = \dfrac{\Delta L_0}{L_0} + 2\dfrac{\Delta d}{d} + \dfrac{\Delta (\Delta L)}{\Delta L}$

Substituting the least counts as absolute errors and the measured values:

$\dfrac{\Delta Y}{Y} = \dfrac{0.1}{150} + 2 \times \dfrac{0.001}{0.08} + \dfrac{0.001}{0.5}$

$\dfrac{\Delta Y}{Y} = \dfrac{1}{1500} + \dfrac{1}{40} + \dfrac{1}{500}$

$\dfrac{\Delta Y}{Y} = \dfrac{2 + 75 + 6}{3000} = \dfrac{83}{3000}$

The absolute error in Young's modulus is:

$\Delta Y = Y \times \dfrac{\Delta Y}{Y} = \left(\dfrac{15}{8\pi} \times 10^{11}\right) \times \dfrac{83}{3000}$

$\Delta Y = \dfrac{15 \times 83}{24000 \pi} \times 10^{11} = \dfrac{83}{1600 \pi} \times 10^{11} = \dfrac{83}{16\pi} \times 10^9 \text{ N/m}^2$

Using $\pi \approx 3.14$:

$\Delta Y = \dfrac{83}{16 \times 3.14} \times 10^9 = \dfrac{83}{50.24} \times 10^9 \approx 1.65 \times 10^9 \text{ N/m}^2$

Comparing this with $\alpha \times 10^9 \text{ N/m}^2$, we get $\alpha = 1.65$.

Answer: $1.65$

subject: Physics
solution:
Young's modulus $Y$ is an intrinsic property of the material of the wire.

It depends only on the nature of the material and its temperature, and is independent of the dimensions of the wire such as length $L$ and radius $r$.

Therefore, when the radius and length of the steel wire are doubled, its Young's modulus remains unchanged.

Answer: remains unchanged

subject: Physics
solution:
From the given graph, we can observe that for a load $W = 20$ N, the extension is $\Delta l = 2 \times 10^{-4}$ m.

The formula for Young's modulus $Y$ is:

$Y = \dfrac{\text{Stress}}{\text{Strain}} = \dfrac{W / A}{\Delta l / L} = \dfrac{W L}{A \Delta l}$

Given:
Length of the wire, $L = 1$ m
Cross-sectional area, $A = 10^{-5}$ m$^2$

Substituting the values into the formula:

$Y = \dfrac{20 \times 1}{10^{-5} \times 2 \times 10^{-4}}$

$Y = \dfrac{20}{2 \times 10^{-9}}$

$Y = 10 \times 10^9 = 1.0 \times 10^{10}$ N/m$^2$

Answer: $1.0 \times 10^{10}$
